summary: American silent film director (1875–1948), widely credited with pioneering modern film language. Best known for The Birth of a Nation (1915), a technically innovative but deeply racist pro-KKK film, and its follow-up Intolerance (1916). Frequent actress Lillian Gish starred in many of his films. Jean-Luc Godard paraphrased his supposed quote that all you need to make a film is 'a girl and a gun.'

works.name: The Birth of a Nation (1915)
works.description: The first film screened at the White House. Based on Thomas Dixon's novel The Clansman. Follows the Cameron and Stoneman families through the Civil War and Reconstruction. African-Americans are 'heroically' stopped from voting. Women are saved from a black militia by the Confederate army. 'The Ride of the Valkyries' plays during the climax as men race to save Lillian Gish's character (Elsie Stoneman) from the doctor Silas Lynch. Raoul Walsh (later a director) played John Wilkes Booth in this film. Widely credited with restarting/reinvigorating the KKK. Wikipedia

clues.clue: First film screened at the White House; follows the Cameron and Stoneman families through Civil War and Reconstruction; Lillian Gish plays Elsie Stoneman
examples: This director followed the Cameron and Stoneman families through the Civil War and Reconstruction in a film that ends with African-Americans 'heroically' being stopped from voting.
That film by this director was the first movie to be screened at the White House.

clues.clue: Based on Thomas Dixon's novel The Clansman; widely credited with restarting the KKK
examples: This director is widely credited with restarting the KKK with his 1915 film Birth of a Nation.
a 1915 epic based on Thomas Dixon's novel The Clansman

clues.clue: 'The Ride of the Valkyries' plays during the climax as men race to save Lillian Gish's character from Silas Lynch
examples: 'The Ride of the Valkyries' plays during the climax of this director's most famous film as men race to save a woman played by Lillian Gish from the doctor Silas Lynch.

clues.clue: Raoul Walsh (later a director) played John Wilkes Booth in this film
examples: The director of The Thief of Bagdad, Raoul Walsh, played John Wilkes Booth in this director's film The Birth of a Nation.

works.name: Intolerance (1916)
works.description: A 'film fugue' (Theodore Huff's term) consisting of four interwoven storylines set in Babylon, Judea (France: St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre), and America, bridged by shots of a woman rocking a cradle (representing Eternal Motherhood). The Babylonian section: Griffith used a 100-foot crane to film women moving down a massive staircase below elephant-topped pillars; a king is delivered a white rose by dove-pulled chariot. The American section: a peon of the 'Musketeer of the Slums' is sentenced to death with the recurring line 'an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth.' The film closes with 'Dare we dream of a golden day when the bestial War shall rule no more.' Made in response to criticism of Birth of a Nation. Jean-Luc Godard paraphrased Griffith's quote ('a girl and a gun') when discussing this director. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Four interwoven storylines (Babylon, Judea, St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre, America) bridged by shots of a woman rocking a cradle (Eternal Motherhood); Theodore Huff called it a 'film fugue'
examples: Theodore Huff called one of this director's films a 'film fugue' for its interweaving of four separate story lines bridged by shots of a woman rocking a cradle.
A film by this director includes shots of a woman rocking a cradle between its storylines, which include the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre and the fall of Babylon.

clues.clue: Babylonian section: Griffith used a 100-foot crane for women moving down a massive staircase below elephant-topped pillars; a king delivered a white rose in a dove-pulled chariot
examples: This director used a 100-foot crane to film women moving in lines down a massive staircase below colossal pillars topped with elephant statues.
In a film by this director, a king is delivered a white rose in a chariot pulled by doves while a man known as the 'Rhapsode' leads an army against him.

clues.clue: American section: a peon of the 'Musketeer of the Slums' is sentenced to death with the recurring line 'an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth'
examples: In an epic by this director, a peon of the 'Musketeer of the Slums' is sentenced to death for murder with the judge proclaiming the recurring line, 'an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth.'

clues.clue: Made in response to criticism of Birth of a Nation; closes with 'Dare we dream of a golden day when the bestial War shall rule no more'
examples: name this director of Broken Blossoms and Intolerance, the latter of which responded to criticism of his Birth of a Nation

An image of the Holy City is shown next to a couple honeymooning by the sea at the end of an epic film by this director that contains the proclamation, 'Dare we dream of a golden day when the bestial War shall rule no more.'

works.name: Broken Blossoms (1919)
works.description: A film about a Chinese missionary known as 'The Yellow Man' who falls in love with Lucy, whose boxer father Battling kills her. In the most famous scene, a girl (Lucy) spins in frantic horror hiding in a closet from her axe-wielding father; she is then beaten to death while The Yellow Man is killed by a Chinese Buddhist missionary. The protagonist shoots the boxer Battling. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A girl (Lucy) spins in frantic horror hiding in a closet from her axe-wielding father; she is beaten to death
examples: In the most famous scene of a film by this director, a girl spins about in frantic horror as she hides in a closet from her axe-wielding father, who is killed by a Chinese Buddhist missionary after beating her to death.

clues.clue: The protagonist 'The Yellow Man' dreams of bringing 'peace to the barbarous Anglo-Saxons, sons of turmoil and strife'; shoots the boxer Battling
examples: One of this man's films describes how its protagonist dreams to 'take the glorious message of peace to the barbarous Anglo-Saxons, sons of turmoil and strife.' That protagonist, who shoots the boxer Battling after he kills his daughter Lucy, is referred to only as 'The Yellow Man.'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: D.W. Griffith (1875–1948), pioneer of classical film language. Jean-Luc Godard paraphrased his supposed quote that 'all you need to make a film is a girl and a gun.' His frequent actress was Lillian Gish (the 'First Lady of the Silent Screen'), who appeared in Birth of a Nation, Broken Blossoms, Way Down East, and Orphans of the Storm. Also directed The Musketeers of Pig Alley (sometimes called the first gangster movie). Godard's Histoire(s) du cinema includes Dorothy and Lillian Gish reacting to a gun from Griffith's An Unseen Enemy. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Jean-Luc Godard paraphrased his supposed quote: 'all you need to make a film is a girl and a gun'
examples: Jean-Luc Godard famously said are the only two things you need to make a film [a girl and a gun]. Godard was paraphrasing a quote by this director of Intolerance and Birth of a Nation.

clues.clue: Frequent actress: Lillian Gish ('First Lady of the Silent Screen'); appeared in Birth of a Nation, Broken Blossoms, Way Down East, and Orphans of the Storm
examples: This actress played Elsie Stoneman in Birth of a Nation. This 'First Lady of the Silent Screen' starred in several of Griffith's films, including Broken Blossoms, Way Down East, and Orphans of the Storm.
Godard's Histoire(s) du cinema includes a shot of these sisters [Dorothy and Lillian Gish] reacting to a gun from Griffith's film An Unseen Enemy.

clues.clue: The Musketeers of Pig Alley (1912) is sometimes called the first gangster movie; first to use follow focus
examples: This short film is believed to have been one of the first to use follow focus... sometimes considered the first gangster movie. The Musketeers of Pig Alley was directed by this guy.

comprehensive_summary:
D.W. Griffith (1875–1948) is widely regarded as a pioneer of classical film language, credited with establishing techniques like cross-cutting, the close-up, and the tracking shot. Jean-Luc Godard paraphrased his supposed quote that 'all you need to make a film is a girl and a gun.' His frequent actress was Lillian Gish (the 'First Lady of the Silent Screen'), who appeared in Birth of a Nation, Broken Blossoms, Way Down East, and Orphans of the Storm.

The Birth of a Nation (1915), based on Thomas Dixon's novel The Clansman, is his most famous — and most notorious — work. It was the first film screened at the White House, and follows the Cameron and Stoneman families through the Civil War and Reconstruction. It ends with African-Americans being 'heroically' prevented from voting. 'The Ride of the Valkyries' plays during the climax as men race to save Lillian Gish's character Elsie Stoneman from Silas Lynch. The film is widely credited with restarting and glamorizing the KKK. Raoul Walsh (later a director) played John Wilkes Booth in the film.

Intolerance (1916) was made in response to the controversy of Birth of a Nation. Theodore Huff called it a 'film fugue': four interwoven stories set in Babylon, Judea, France (the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre), and America, bridged throughout by shots of a woman rocking a cradle (representing Eternal Motherhood). For the Babylonian section, Griffith used a 100-foot crane to film women moving down a massive staircase beneath colossal pillars topped with elephant statues; a king is delivered a white rose in a dove-pulled chariot. The American section features the recurring line 'an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth.' The film ends with 'Dare we dream of a golden day when the bestial War shall rule no more.'

Broken Blossoms (1919) features a Chinese missionary ('The Yellow Man') who falls in love with Lucy, whose father Battling beats her to death. The most famous scene depicts Lucy spinning in frantic horror hiding in a closet from her axe-wielding father.

The Musketeers of Pig Alley (1912) is sometimes called the first gangster film and believed to be the first to use follow focus.
